food_subgroup: Squid (Loligo spp.), raw, cleaned (tube and tentacles)

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for raw cleaned squid in available reference; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient for this record.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium cleaned squid (tube and tentacles) weighs approximately 125g and provides around 115 kcal, 19.5g protein, and 1.75g fat.

nutrient_source.title: Mollusks, squid, mixed species, raw — USDA SR Legacy, NDB No. 15149; cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F03050021
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values sourced from USDA SR Legacy NDB No. 15149 (Mollusks, squid, mixed species, raw) — an approved primary source. Values cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo Database Food Code F03050021 (exact match). Both approved sources are in close agreement for all core macros and secondary nutrients. USDA SR Legacy is used as the declared primary source to satisfy g2 approved-source compliance requirements.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — food contains negligible digestible carbohydrates
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (consulted; no GI value listed for raw squid as GI testing is not performed on negligible-carbohydrate animal foods)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Raw cleaned squid contains only 3.1g carbohydrate per 100g, of which 0g is free sugar and 0g is dietary fibre; the carbohydrate present is primarily glycogen which is rapidly metabolised and does not produce a meaningful glycemic response. The University of Sydney GI Database does not list a GI value for raw squid as GI testing is not applicable to negligible-carbohydrate animal-protein foods. glycemic_index set to null per Veritas rules for low-carbohydrate seafood. gi_category set to not_applicable. glycemic_load_per_serving set to 0.0.

hypertension.guidance: Raw squid is low in sodium (44mg/100g) and provides potassium (246mg/100g), supporting a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio; however, patients should be cautioned that common preparations such as sambal sotong, salt-and-pepper squid, and soy-sauce-based stir-fries substantially increase sodium intake and should be consumed in moderation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (3.1g/100g), high lean protein (15.6g/100g), negligible sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw squid is an excellent protein source for individuals with type 2 diabetes, providing 15.6g protein and only 3.1g carbohydrate per 100g with zero free sugar, resulting in minimal postprandial glycemic impact; the exceptional selenium content (44.8mcg/100g) may additionally support antioxidant defence relevant to diabetes-associated oxidative stress.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary cholesterol (233mg/100g) but very low saturated fat (0.4g/100g); lean protein source
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Despite its high dietary cholesterol content (233mg/100g), raw squid is very low in saturated fat (0.4g/100g) and total fat (1.4g/100g); current evidence indicates that dietary cholesterol from squid has limited effect on blood LDL in most individuals, and its lean protein profile makes it a heart-healthy seafood choice when prepared without added saturated fats or excessive sodium.

rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Raw squid provides exceptionally high selenium (44.8mcg/100g), which serves as a co-factor for glutathione peroxidase, an antioxidant enzyme that neutralises free radicals implicated in joint inflammation associated with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Squid (sotong in Malay) is a cephalopod mollusc of the genus Loligo widely consumed across Singapore, Southeast Asia, and the broader Indo-Pacific region. The cleaned form used in cooking consists of the cylindrical mantle (tube) and the curled tentacles, with the ink sac, transparent pen (gladius), and beak removed. The flesh is translucent white with a purplish-grey outer skin that is typically peeled before cooking. Raw squid has a firm, slightly slippery texture that becomes tender and slightly chewy when cooked quickly over high heat; overcooking causes it to become rubbery. Nutritionally, raw cleaned squid is a very lean, high-protein food providing 15.6g protein and only 1.4g fat per 100g at 92 kcal. It is notably high in dietary cholesterol (233mg/100g), but its saturated fat content is minimal (0.4g/100g), and current clinical evidence indicates that squid cholesterol has limited impact on blood LDL for most individuals. The most clinically significant micronutrient is selenium (44.8mcg/100g), which is among the highest of commonly consumed Southeast Asian seafood and supports glutathione peroxidase antioxidant activity. Squid also provides meaningful phosphorus (221mg), potassium (246mg), riboflavin (0.41mg), and vitamin B12 (1.3mcg). In Singapore cuisine, the most iconic preparation is sambal sotong — squid stir-fried in a spicy sambal paste of chillies, shrimp paste, and aromatics. Other common preparations include stir-fried squid with black bean sauce, salt-and-pepper squid, squid ink noodles, hot pot, and squid soup. The glycemic index is not applicable as carbohydrate content is negligible (3.1g/100g, primarily glycogen with no free sugars).
meal_context: stir-fried squid with black bean sauce

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA SR Legacy NDB No. 15149 (Mollusks, squid, mixed species, raw) — an approved primary source — cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo Database Food Code F03050021 (exact match). Both approved primary sources are in close agreement for all core macros and micronutrients. All mandatory fields are fully populated from verified data.
notes: sugar_g is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy NDB No. 15149 and HPB Singapore Foodo Database F03050021 — raw squid contains no free sugars. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as squid is an animal-derived food with no plant cell-wall material. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo Database F03050021. vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo Database F03050021 (raw squid is not a significant dietary vitamin D source). monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data not consistently reported across both primary sources; to avoid introducing cross-source uncertainty, these values are withheld pending a single-source confirmation. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo Database F03050021 primary record; USDA SR Legacy reports approximately 78.6g water per 100g for mixed-species squid raw but this has not been adopted as the primary source for this field pending HPB confirmation. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (3.1g/100g, primarily glycogen with no free sugars); gi_category set to not_applicable per Veritas GI rules for low-carbohydrate seafood. Dietary cholesterol is high (233mg/100g) but saturated fat is very low (0.4g/100g); clinical guidance reflects current evidence that squid cholesterol has limited LDL-raising effect. Allergen note: squid is classified as a mollusc allergen; individuals with shellfish or mollusc allergies should avoid. Tamil local_name updated to கணவாய் (பச்சை, சுத்தம் செய்யப்பட்ட) — கணவாய் is the standard Tamil term for squid; kanavaai is the romanized form included in aliases. Aliases self-check performed: all strings in aliases_by_language verified present verbatim in flat aliases array.
